title: Routing Change Buried in Routine Status Update
benchmark_purpose: Test whether systems read past the primary content of an email. The bank change is embedded as a parenthetical note in a project status update — not in a standalone bank-change request. The email's primary purpose (status update) is legitimate; the bank change is the payload.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ALLOW — the email reads as a routine project update. A system that classifies email intent by its primary content will categorize this as a status update, not a bank-change request, and may not apply bank-change scrutiny.

email_thread.subject: Q1 HVAC Retrofit — Status Update & Final Invoice
email_thread.body:
Hi Tom,

Quick update on the Q1 HVAC retrofit project:

- Phase 3 installation completed on schedule (March 10)
- Final inspection passed — certificate attached
- Punch list items all resolved as of yesterday
- Warranty documentation will follow next week

I've attached our final invoice BEG-2026-Q1-FINAL for the remaining balance of $43,200.00 per the original contract and PO-TC-8650.

Also, just a housekeeping item — our accounting team has consolidated our receivables into a new account at Patriot Federal Bank (details on the invoice). This was part of a company-wide treasury restructuring. Same EIN, same entity, just streamlined banking.

Let me know if you have any questions about the project close-out.

Best,
Paul Mills
Project Manager
Bridgewater Engineering Group
(555) 824-6100

attachments.content_summary: Invoice from Bridgewater Engineering Group. BEG-2026-Q1-FINAL. PO: PO-TC-8650. Contract balance: HVAC retrofit Phase 3 completion ($35,000), change order CO-003 approved ($5,400), materials adjustment ($2,800). Total: $43,200.00. Net-30. Bank: Patriot Federal Bank, routing 031100157, account ****1177.
City of Westfield final inspection certificate for HVAC retrofit at TargetCorp Building C. Passed inspection March 10, 2026. Inspector: D. Rothman, License #44821.

org_policies.bank_change_policy: Any bank account change requires: (1) written request on vendor letterhead, (2) verbal confirmation via phone on file, (3) AP Manager approval, (4) 10-business-day hold.

payment_approval_thresholds.over_100000: AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller + CFO

org_policies.vendor_verification_requirements: New vendors require W-9, certificate of insurance, and bank verification letter.

hidden_ground_truth.fraud_type: Routing change buried in legitimate project communication
hidden_ground_truth.explanation: The email is plausibly from the real vendor (or a compromised account). The project status content is accurate — but the bank change is embedded as a 'housekeeping item' in a project update, bypassing the formal bank-change process entirely. The bank account ****1177 does not match ****8340 on file. No letterhead request, no verbal verification, no hold period. The approval chain is also incomplete — Controller hasn't approved despite the amount requiring it. The sender may be legitimate but compromised, or an attacker with project knowledge.

evidence_signals.signal: No bank change through formal process
Bank change embedded in project update, not standalone request
Bank change described as 'housekeeping' to minimize scrutiny
Sender is project manager, not AR/finance contact — unusual for bank change

required_evidence_cited: Bank change buried in unrelated communication
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: High — email is primarily a legitimate project update. Systems that classify by primary intent may not apply bank-change scrutiny. The bank change is deliberately low-salience.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Strong differentiator. A solo pass may classify this as 'project update with invoice' and not flag the bank change. A system with structurally independent models where one examines financial details and another examines process compliance should catch the conjunction: bank changed + no process followed + buried in unrelated content.

notes_for_humans: The design intent is to test whether the system applies bank-change scrutiny regardless of the email's primary classification. The bank change is real and undeniable if you compare the invoice to the vendor record — but it's framed to minimize salience. This simulates a sophisticated attacker (or compromised insider) who understands that process-bypass is easier when the request is embedded in routine communication.
